items.question: I don't repair cars myself. I ___ .
explanations: Correct! Causative = have + object + participle: 'have my car repaired'.
'Have repaired my car' means you did it yourself.
'Repair' is the base verb; use the participle 'repaired'.
'Repair my car' means you do it yourself.

items.question: She ___ last week — it looks great and short now.
explanations: 'Cut her hair' means she did it herself.
Correct! A hairdresser did it: 'She had her hair cut'.
'Had cut her hair' is past perfect active — she did it.
'Has her hair cut' is present, but 'last week' needs the past.

items.question: We're ___ next week — the walls are looking tired.
explanations: Correct! Continuous causative: 'having the house painted'.
'Painting had the house' is not correct word order.
'Have the house paint' uses the base verb.
'Having painted the house' means you painted it yourself.

items.question: Choose the informal version of 'I had my phone fixed.'
explanations: Correct! 'Get' is the informal equivalent: 'I got my phone fixed'.
'Get fix my phone' has the wrong structure.
'Had fixed my phone' means you did it yourself.
'Fixed my phone' means you did it yourself.

items.question: They ___ at the airport — someone took it from the carousel.
explanations: Correct! Something happened to them: 'had their bag stolen'.
'Stole their bag' means they did the stealing.
'Had stolen their bag' is past perfect active — they stole it.
'Have their bag steal' uses the base verb.

items.question: I need to ___ before my trip.
explanations: Correct! Causative with 'get': 'get my passport renewed'.
'Get renew my passport' has the wrong structure.
'Renew got my passport' is not correct.
'Renew' is the base verb; use 'renewed'.

items.question: Correct this: 'He had his tooth remove yesterday.'
options: He had his tooth removed yesterday.
He had removed his tooth yesterday.
He has his tooth remove yesterday.
explanations: Correct! Use the past participle: 'had his tooth removed'.
'Had removed his tooth' means he did it himself.
'Has ... remove' uses the wrong tense and base verb.
'Remove' should be the participle 'removed'.

items.question: What does 'I had my windows cleaned' mean?
options: Someone cleaned the windows for me.
explanations: Correct! The causative means someone did it for you.
That would be 'I cleaned my windows'.
This is past, not future.
Windows don't clean themselves.

items.question: Put the tense correctly: 'She ___ her eyes tested every year.'
explanations: Correct! A yearly habit takes the present: 'She has her eyes tested every year'.
'Having' can't stand alone as the main verb here.
'Had been' doesn't fit a present habit.
'Is have' is not correct English.

items.question: Which sentence means you did the work YOURSELF?
explanations: Correct! 'I painted the fence' — you did it.
'Had the fence painted' means someone did it for you.
'Got the fence painted' means someone did it for you.
'Having the fence painted' means someone is doing it for you.
